title: Postgres connection pooling with PgBouncer
content: Notes on database performance. We put PgBouncer in front of Postgres in transaction pooling mode to cap the number of backend connections. Each API pod opened its own pool, which exhausted Postgres max_connections under load. PgBouncer in transaction mode lets thousands of client connections share a small set of server connections. Watch out for session-level features like prepared statements and advisory locks, which break under transaction pooling.

title: Kubernetes liveness vs readiness probes
content: Operations runbook entry. A liveness probe tells Kubernetes whether to restart a container; a readiness probe tells it whether to send traffic. We had a service flapping because its liveness probe hit a slow dependency, so Kubernetes kept restarting a healthy pod. The fix was to make liveness a cheap in-process check and put the dependency check in readiness instead, so the pod is removed from the load balancer without being killed.

title: Rust async cancellation and select!
content: Engineering note about Tokio. When you use tokio::select! and one branch completes, the other futures are dropped, which cancels them at the next await point. This bit us when a database write was cancelled halfway through a select against a timeout, leaving an inconsistent row. The lesson: don't run state-mutating work directly inside a select branch unless it is cancellation-safe; spawn it as a task and select on its JoinHandle instead.

title: Choosing a vector database for semantic search
content: Architecture decision record. We compared Qdrant, Weaviate, and pgvector for our semantic search feature. pgvector kept everything in Postgres but struggled past a few million vectors. Qdrant gave us payload filtering, hybrid dense plus sparse search, and horizontal scaling, so we chose it. We run it self-hosted with a snapshot backup to object storage every night.

title: Espresso dial-in for a light roast
content: Personal coffee log. Dialing in a light Ethiopian roast on the espresso machine. Started at an 18 gram dose, 36 gram yield, 28 second shot and it was sour and grassy. Going finer on the grind and pushing the yield to 40 grams over 32 seconds rounded it out. Light roasts want a higher brew temperature, around 94 Celsius, and a longer pre-infusion than the darker roasts I usually pull.

title: Marathon training week 14 long run
content: Running journal. Week 14 of the marathon block. Did a 32 kilometer long run at an easy aerobic pace, fueling with a gel every 40 minutes. Legs felt strong through 26 km, then the last 6 were a grind into a headwind. Heart rate drifted up about 8 beats by the end, which is normal cardiac drift for a run that long. Two weeks until taper starts.

title: Sourdough hydration and open crumb
content: Baking notes. Trying to get a more open crumb on the country sourdough. Bumped hydration from 72 to 78 percent and added a round of coil folds during bulk. The higher hydration dough was slack and hard to shape but baked up with much bigger, glossier holes. Key was a longer bulk ferment until the dough was jiggly and domed, not flat.

title: Tax treatment of RSUs at vesting
content: Personal finance memo. Restricted stock units are taxed as ordinary income at vesting, based on the share price that day, and the company usually withholds shares to cover it. Any gain after vesting is a capital gain when you eventually sell, short or long term depending on how long you hold. I should set aside extra because the default withholding rate often under-withholds for higher brackets.

title: Negotiating a SaaS enterprise contract
content: Procurement notes. When negotiating the enterprise analytics contract, the list price was an anchor, not the real price. We got 30 percent off by committing to two years and agreeing to be a reference customer. The important non-price terms were a data processing addendum, an SLA with service credits, and a cap on annual price increases at renewal.

title: JWT authentication and refresh tokens
content: Security design note. Our API uses short-lived JSON Web Tokens for access, around 15 minutes, plus a long-lived refresh token stored as an httpOnly secure cookie. The access token is a bearer credential, so we keep it out of localStorage to limit XSS exposure. Refresh tokens are rotated on each use and revoked server-side if reuse is detected, which catches token theft.

title: CSS grid vs flexbox for page layout
content: Frontend guideline. Use CSS grid for two-dimensional layout where you control both rows and columns, like a page shell or a card gallery. Use flexbox for one-dimensional flow, like a toolbar or a row of buttons that should wrap. Nesting a flexbox inside a grid cell is common and fine. Avoid absolute positioning for layout; reserve it for overlays and tooltips.

title: Debugging a memory leak in a Node service
content: Incident writeup. A Node.js service slowly grew to its memory limit and got OOM-killed every few hours. Heap snapshots showed an ever-growing array of event listeners. The cause was registering a listener on a shared emitter inside a request handler without ever removing it. Moving the registration to startup and reusing one handler fixed the leak and flattened the memory graph.

title: Lisbon trip itinerary and neighborhoods
content: Travel plan. Five days in Lisbon. Staying in Alfama for the old-town feel and the miradouros at sunset. Day trips planned to Sintra for the palaces and to Cascais by train along the coast. Eating pasteis de nata in Belem and seafood in Cais do Sodre. Booking the tram 28 early in the morning to beat the crowds. Budget is mostly food and a couple of museum tickets.

title: Garden tomato blight prevention
content: Gardening log. The tomatoes got early blight last summer, with brown spots and yellowing lower leaves. This year I am spacing the plants further apart for airflow, mulching to stop soil splashing onto leaves, watering at the base in the morning, and pruning the lowest branches. If it shows up again I will rotate the bed and try a copper-based spray early.

title: Designing an idempotent payment webhook
content: Backend design note. Payment provider webhooks can be delivered more than once, so the handler must be idempotent. We store the provider event id in a unique column and treat a duplicate insert as an already-processed no-op. The actual order fulfillment runs inside the same transaction as recording the event id, so we never fulfill twice or fulfill without recording.

title: Choosing running shoes for a neutral gait
content: Gear note. For a neutral runner who overpronates only mildly, a daily trainer with moderate stack and a bit of guidance works better than a heavy motion-control shoe. I rotate a soft max-cushion shoe for recovery runs and a lighter, firmer shoe for tempo work. Replacing them around 600 to 800 kilometers once the midsole foam stops rebounding.

title: Setting up OpenTelemetry tracing
content: Observability runbook. We instrumented the services with OpenTelemetry, exporting traces via OTLP to a collector that fans out to our tracing backend. The biggest win was propagating the trace context across the message queue, so an async job shows up in the same trace as the request that enqueued it. Sampling is head-based at 10 percent with a rule to always keep errors.

title: French press vs pour over flavor
content: Coffee comparison. Same beans, two methods. The French press uses a metal mesh, so more oils and fine sediment get through, giving a heavier, rounder body. The pour over with a paper filter traps the oils, producing a cleaner, brighter, more tea-like cup that shows off the acidity. For a fruity light roast I prefer the pour over; for a chocolatey dark roast the press.

title: Postmortem culture and blameless reviews
content: Team process doc. We run blameless postmortems after every significant incident. The goal is to fix the system, not to find someone to blame, because people hide information when they fear punishment. Each postmortem has a timeline, contributing factors, what went well, and concrete action items with owners and due dates. We review open action items at the start of the next one.

title: Strength training for runners
content: Fitness note. Adding two strength sessions a week to support marathon training. Focus on single-leg work like split squats and step-ups, plus hip and calf strength to handle the repetitive load of running. Heavy and low-rep builds tendon stiffness and running economy without adding much bulk. Keeping the hard lifting on the same days as hard runs so easy days stay easy.

title: GDPR data subject access requests
content: Compliance note. Under GDPR a data subject can request a copy of their personal data and we must respond within one month. We built an export endpoint that gathers a user's records across services into a single archive. We also handle erasure requests, which delete or anonymize the data while keeping records we are legally required to retain, like invoices.

title: Migrating from REST to GraphQL
content: Architecture note. We added a GraphQL gateway in front of existing REST services to let the mobile app fetch exactly the fields it needs in one round trip, fixing over-fetching and under-fetching. The tricky part was the N+1 query problem, solved with DataLoader batching. We kept REST for server-to-server calls where a fixed contract is simpler than a flexible query.

title: Choosing a standing desk and chair
content: Home office note. Bought an electric sit-stand desk with memory presets so I can switch posture through the day. Pairing it with an ergonomic chair with adjustable lumbar and armrests. The rule of thumb is elbows at 90 degrees, monitor top at eye level, and feet flat. Alternating sitting and standing every 30 to 45 minutes beats standing all day, which just trades one ache for another.

title: Backpacking meal planning and weight
content: Outdoors note. Planning food for a four-day backpacking trip, aiming for about 700 grams of food per day to balance calories against pack weight. Dinners are dehydrated meals rehydrated with boiling water; lunches are tortillas, hard cheese, and cured meat; breakfast is oats with milk powder. Repackaging everything out of bulky retail packaging into ziplocks saves surprising amounts of space and weight.
